channel.title: Worldbuilding on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Worldbuilding on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Self-Consistent Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about self-consistent lore.

I’ve discussed realism and lore actually in a couple different videos. One I compared realism to fun and talked about how they’re not the same thing and I never consider them the same thing And for me fun trumps realism. So I’ll link that video in the description. But I’ve also talked about how realism isn’t even necessary. Um, it’s something that sometimes people comment on like this isn’t realistic and they’re playing a fantasy game with dragons and spells. I think part of the problem is realism gets conflated with consistency.

item.title: Let's Talk About Canon
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, let’s talk about Canon.

It’s a fun Friday. I want to talk about it, talk about different things, how I use it here, A lot of comments that I get about it And also, yeah, I see some boxes in the background.

Don’t worry about that, It’s that’s not what I’m talking about today. Just some boxes, some empty shelves.

Let’s move on, so. A lot of talking about canon on my channel and that’s what I want to talk about. Like, what is it? Who gets to decide it? What happens when people disagree? Wow, I see a lot of discussion of this.

item.title: Leaving Some Mystery
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about leaving some mystery in your games.

I almost called this building a mystery, and then I was going to have a guitar and an onk necklace and I was going to do that Sarah McLachlan song I know you’re thinking of. But let’s just say that it didn’t test well with the focus group and it would leave me open to copyright disputes. So you’re getting leaving a mystery. What do I mean by that? This is something that’s come up in a lot of my videos and it came up recently in um.

item.title: Non-Expository Arcanum Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about non-expository Arcanum lore.

This is a companion video to the non-expository Fallout lore that I did a while back, where Fath Daniel asked: “What is Arcanum, non-expository lore” And I thought, “Yeah, I should do that because that makes sense” And I It’s something that it’s interesting. Um, it was harder for me to do this for Arcanum than for Fallout And because Fallout was a smaller game in sense of scope, number of NPCs, number of dialogues, cutscenes, in-game books — So it was harder for me to find Arcanum lore that had never been, that had never appeared in the game already. Like, there’s a cut scene that mentions the lore or an NPC talks about it at length, and we had several NPCs that could talk about a lot of lore that you may not know, because it was hidden behind a, speech skill, or you had to have a certain reactivity level with them, or you just never dove down. In their dialogue tree to find it. And then there were lots and lots and lots of books. So I tried to find lore for Arcanum.

item.title: Non-Expository Fallout Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, for a fun Friday, I wanted to talk about nonexpository Fallout lore.

Let me explain what I mean by that. First of all, I’m talking about lore from the first game only, not even from Fallout 2.. I’m talking about lore that the original team discussed among ourselves and decided was true, and then we made the game based on that. We never wrote this stuff down. In fact, there weren’t a lot of design documents back in the 90s. I think the vision document was one of the few truly laid out, organized, specific documents we wrote.

item.title: Fallout Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout lore exploration.

This comes from a really good question from Joseé Luis Lopez 7511, who asked: “What part of the Fallout universe needs to be expanded upon or what story or stories deserve to be continued? Perhaps take a bit from each game and weave the evolution of the formula”. This is an interesting question. Um, I debated whether to answer it because whenever I talk about Fallout, some people go crazy. But there’s a lot of me in Fallout. I’ve said this a lot.

item.title: Anemoia
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about anamoya, which is feeling nostalgia for a time and place that you’ve never known — And yes

, I had to look that term up. You see, I watch a lot of documentaries. What would be a Tim Kaine video without a story? So I was watching a particular documentary on Roman buildings that have been continuously occupied since they were built centuries, millennia ago. And it was just really interesting to me because I — I I don’t really, you know, want to live in an old Roman building, but it was an interesting documentary And they were showing different buildings and showing what they had originally been built for and what they were be being used for now and how much of the original uh architecture was still exposed, because sometimes they had been plastered over And also some of the buildings were used for different things, but a lot of times they focused on buildings that were still being used for exactly what they had been built to be. And they happened to mention a building in Capidoshia, which, of course, made me think of this. But I suppose I should thank you. After all, it led me to my true calling, Ceil.

item.title: Sci-Fi vs. High Fantasy Settings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about sci-fi versus high F fantasy settings, specifically in a new IP.

This comes from a question from Hungry Hungry Hobo 196, who asked: hey, Tim, if you had your choice to make a new crpg, would you rather do a high fantasy or sci-fi? How do you feel about sci-fi with fantasy elements such as magic, and how hard is it to make a game that has guns or lasers and make a sword feel viable? Those are good questions and once again I find myself going: haven’t I already talked about that and I looked, and I haven’t talked about it in one of my videos, but this is a perfect example of the kind of discussions I’ve had with many other game developers. We talk about things like this all the time, and what’s fun about talking with your fellow game developers is you’re not trying to con like someone, like a publisher or someone above you, to make a game this could be like. A lunchtime thing where you’re just sitting around talking and you’re like, yeah, you know what I prefer between these two and here’s why. And then there’s a spirited discussion of the topic and no one’s necessarily trying to convince anyone, you’re just trying to go. Well, here’s what I think blah. I love discussions like that because it helps me kind of hash out my own feelings on the topic.

item.title: Arcanum Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. I want to talk to you today about Arcanum lore exploration.

What I mean by that is kind of a deep dive into some of the Arcanum lore that interests me. Now I did a video already on the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum, and there’s a video about it. I’ll link it below, and in that there was this idea for combining magic and tech. There was an ore that let you make Tech ological items that you could magically imbue, and I didn’t like it then. And now, 25 years later, I absolutely hate the idea, and the reason for that is I think it takes a fundamental pillar of the Arcanum setting, which is Magic and Tech are diametrically opposed, not because they don’t like each other, not because they don’t get along and you need to find, you know, a substrate to get them, you know, happy to be together because they work diametrically oppositely.

item.title: A World Building Tip
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give you a World building tip and of course, before I do it, I kind of want to give you context and then I’ll give you the tip, which is a one sentence tip, and then I’ll talk about it at length.

So I’ve already talked in other videos about making settings. I have a whole video about making a good one, and it pretty much consists of questions you should be asking yourself as you put together your setting. I’ve got another video on a technique to make a setting, which I call my what if? Method. Which is You think of something that’s very grounded in reality, but then you say: what if I change this one thing?

item.title: Naming Things
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about naming things.

What I mean by that is, when you start to put together a game you’re going to have to come up with so many names you’re going to have to name the game. You’re going to have to name items in the game, places in the game, other people in the game. Uh, if it’s a space game, you’re going to have to come up with — you know fanciful technology names and names for other aliens. If it’s a fantasy game, going to have to come up with magical sounding names and you know names that describe magical processes, like: oh no, the wizard has placed Wards on all of the glyph Stones. Whatever, what I want to talk about is how to come up with those names, and I think this process is something that’s often overlooked, and I’m going to share a couple examples of things — well known, I think, that are just off. I’m going to talk about how I did it. Now I’m going to start, though, with saying I’m kind of up and down with names, especially proper names.

item.title: Converting A Movie Into A Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about converting, like a movie or TV show into a game.

I’m going to use an example. This is to answer a question from Andrew Williams, zn4, NP, who asked: uh, here’s a question idea. Have you ever watched a movie or show and thought that would make a fun game? If so, what movie or show would you do?

item.title: Galactic Coordinates
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. It’s Friday, Funday, so I wanted to pull something out of my idea book about Galactic coordinates.

This was a system that I made years ago, decades ago. Uh, it’s in my first book, which means it was at least — it’s at least- 15 years old, it’s. I needed a system, or I wanted a system, to name Stellar systems, son, planets, moons. I pulled this out of the idea book about eight years ago when we started working on The Outer Worlds, because I thought, well, maybe I finally have a chance to use this. Didn’t end up using it. We went with another system, um, and then, ironically, it never came up in the game. So the system for I for cataloging Stellar system in The Outer Worlds hasn’t yet hit lore.

item.title: Thinking "What If?" In Detail
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about a technique I call thinking what if in detail?

This is primarily for setting designers out there, because, remember I said, the order I like design to go in is setting, followed by story, followed by System mechanics. This is a technique that I use to come up with new settings, and I’ll talk about that in a few minutes. But what I want to start with is: this came because I was looking at my notes, like I’m always doing, and there was a note about a lunch meeting at troa. It wasn’t an official meeting, it was a bunch of us were sitting around — I think it was during Temple. A bunch of us are sitting around eating lunch and someone had brought up trying to guess, um, what people from a century ago would be amazed at. And things were coming up like you know, cars, although cars have been around kind of for at least a century now, but more things like all the appliances. We have, you know: washers, dryers, refrigerators, the fact that we can have ice whenever we want, um, the fact that travel is a lot easier, um, and more common.

item.title: Games Predicting Our Future
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about games predicting our future.

In particular, I want to ask, I want to answer several questions that G works AKA Nicole asked: I’ve been curious for years how all of you creators get that close to the truth and our reality. Just how do you, one, come up with the ideas for the story of your games? Two, what things in current life help you come up with the future in games? And three, do you have some crystal ball joke?

item.title: Using Mechanics For Storytelling
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using Mechanics for storytelling, and

this is an answer to a question by Benjamin nun 11. Can you talk about gaming gameplay mechanics as storytelling devices? By that I mean where gameplay mechanic is directly reinforcing a character’s connection with the setting or the story.

So, uh, Benjamin, I love this question. It’s the main reason I like to do mechanic after deciding the setting and story, because I think mechanics should support those. Did a video about people who do mechanics.

item.title: An Example Of Making A New IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making an IP an actual example.

Like I’ve talked about making and maintaining an IP, I’ve talked about the order to do it, in which is setting, story, system mechanics. I’ve talked about making what goes into a good setting. I’ve even talked about what your design doc should look like. Those are all videos I’ll link below.

Today, let’s do it. Let’s actually take something from one of my design idea books. I will talk about it, I will expound on it, I’ll show how the idea gets converted into an actual design that you can say now it’s an IP. So, and I will do it in the recommended order, talk about setting, story and systems. So my idea from this book was: I wanted to make a post-apocalyptic game — yeah, another post-apocalyptic game, but I didn’t want it to be nuclear war or any of the other things I’d use before. I had a new idea and what was going to be. Different about this one is: uh, a lot of people are gone, a lot of monsters, and I wanted to come up with more variation than what I got to do on Fallout, which was: here are some gos and giant bugs and mutants.

item.title: Lore Drift
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about lore drift.

I’ve seen people complain online, especially recently, about lore changing. Um, yeah, I’m talking Fallout lot, but I also I’m talking about this for a lot of ips. I mean, it’s not just Fallout people talk about this, it’s um, Star Trek, uh Dune. For me it was the Conan stuff when Conan went from being a book to a movie. And I want to talk about all this.

item.title: Making Good Factions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about making good factions for your games.

This is something that every time I go into making a setting and remember — I think setting is the first step of what you need to do to make a game — I always try to think of what kind of factions are going to be there. And just to be clear what I’m going to talk about, I’m talking about designing the factions, not writing dialogues for particular characters. I’m not good at that — but I am talking about making factions that you’ve designed so that they seem like they belong in your world and have their own goals and are just fun to interact with. I’m going to talk about how I think you should go about doing that.

item.title: Game Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game lore.

I want to answer a question from armored spaz, who asks: how do you keep players informed about the game world, and how could you, how could the game be designed to make the player want to find out more about the game? I think this is a really good question. I almost didn’t answer it though, because it kind of moves into the narrative design territory that I have said in the past, I’m not that good at. But I think I’m good at thinking about narrative design. I’m just not good at implementing narrative design, so let’s talk about that also.

item.title: Lord Of Light CRPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer another question, this one from aramel whisperwind, who asks.

I read Lord of Light a couple days ago, based on your recommendation in one of your earlier videos. Now I’m sitting here thinking about your notes and how it would translate into a crpg. Perhaps you could talk about that.

Okay, my favorite book — this is my Eastman press copy, which is gilded and leather bound, smells really good. So I looked through that and looked through some of my notes and this is what I came up with. So, first of all, I’ve always talked about the three important things about any RPG that you design first, which is setting, mechanics and story. So let’s talk about what I would do for those three things for Lord of Light. First of all, setting. I think this setting very much needs to support exploration by the player. That’s what you want to do in an RPG. You want to get out there, so what I would? Do is I’d go through the book and look, at all the really cool places that are mentioned and sometimes only lightly touched upon but never really fleshed out by zelazny. For example, he mentions a bunch of cities, but he barely talks about what’s in them.

item.title: Setting Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about setting ideas.

I’ve talked about brainstorming, I’ve talked about doing design documents and that I always start with the setting before I do story and then I do system mechanics. So I thought I’d just talk about ideas for settings, kind of going to walk you through, kind of how I do my notebooks, kind of ties into brainstorming. But I’m just going to talk about like kind of what I even do now and how I come up with setting ideas.

item.title: Arcanum: Explaining The Void
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the void in Arcanum and this is something that somebody asked about.

Somebody asked if I could talk about the void because it was mysterious and they wouldn’t know the back story and how we came up with it and if there were any plans to use it in future games, so I know that the void came up um as an idea in Arcanum because it kind of filled the void of answering questions for two different things. The first one was when we made the list of magic spells, there was one that lets you speak with dead. We originally envisioned it as how speak with dad works in AD or DND version one, whatever you call that — where you could ask a set number of questions and the higher level you were, the more questions you could ask.

item.title: The Origin of Fallout's Setting
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the beginning, the creation of the origin of the Fallout setting.

If you remember what I said in my video about design documents, it all starts with setting, and I want to talk about that. What prompted me to think about this was, uh, somebody I know who’s in the industry — I’ve never worked with them — said I’d be interested to know more about how Fallout setting got created, and I thought I had mentioned it. But looking back, I’ve dropped little bits and pieces here and there in other videos, so I’m gonna try to make you a timeline of what happened. To create this timeline, I had to do a lot of research. I had both digital and actual packed away notes and why. Going through those boxes. That’s when I found this shirt, which I don’t think I’ve worn in five years, so minimum, maybe longer.

item.title: How To Make A Good Game Setting
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to make a good setting for computer games.

I’m not going to talk about the structure of the design document. I want to talk about, like, how you actually develop a good setting, and this was actually really hard for me to do. I, I started and stopped writing down things about this video several times because I wasn’t sure how I did this in the past. I — I don’t want to say I — stumbled into a good setting. There was obviously a process I went through, but it wasn’t necessarily conscious. I likened it to excuse me, when you ride a bike or when you’re learning to need bread, you know when you’ve reached a point where you can stop needing it, but it’s really hard to describe. I’ve seen people talk about pinching it and stretching It’s like for me, it’s like you’re needing it and then you, it feels a certain way and you’re like I can stop, kind of like making a setting for a game. I work on it and it gets a certain way and I’m like, okay, that’s a good setting.

item.title: My Favorite Sci Fi Novel
item.description:
Hi, it’s Tim. I’m back with the answer to the first topic that, uh, someone had a question about on my last video, and that was user Hyperion said that

I had mentioned that Walter Miller’s book I can’t go of Leibowitz was really influential on the design of Fallout setting asked what other sci-fi books I liked — um, of course I have a lot of books. Um, this is just a fraction of them. Uh, growing up in the 70s, obviously, Arthur C Clarke, Robert Heinlein, Isaac Asimov, Ursula the Gwynne were just hugely influential. When I got older, though, as a young man I guess I read more of Dan Simmons Hyperion series, Ian Banks culture novels, Rogers zelazny’s Amber novels — um, those are the kind of books I really liked to read. But I do have one absolute Freight favorite book of all time, and that would be Lord of Light by Roger zlosny. I love this book, I love it, I love it has everything I want in a book. It’s well written. It’s non-linear, it incorporates. It kind of reads as a fantasy book, but then it turns into a Sci-Fi novel, incorporates religion into the story in a really seamless way. It very gradually reveals what the world is, which I really like. I don’t like front loading everything on you. Um, I love this book so much that I not only own — this is my science fiction book club — but I’ve caught an early edition and I have the Easton press leather bound copy. That’s the only book I think I own multiple copies of.
